english_query: counterexample to: A person possesses intellectual humility if and only if (i) the person has an accurate assessment of the limits of the person's own knowledge and cognitive abilities, (ii) the person does not claim greater epistemic authority than is warranted by the person's actual competence, and (iii) the person is disposed to acknowledge the person's own errors and to credit the contributions of others.
ugf_response: First we see that a person can choose to help or not help. Then we note that many think about this choice. One thought is that helping gives more good things for the group, so the person will help. Another thought is that it is okay for a person to be used like a thing for the group. But the feeling of being used can be strong when the good for the group is very big and the bad for the person is small. Even if the good for many may still be bigger than the hurt for the single person. Some might say that hurting yourself is better because you get help now. Still the most idea says no to help because each person is also thinking about the good for all. So my answer is: No help should be done unless the good for each person is very big; it is best to keep helping.

english_query: counterexample to: An omission O by a person P is the legal cause of a harm H if and only if (i) P had a duty to act, (ii) H would not have occurred had P fulfilled that duty, and (iii) P's failure to act was the sole cause of H.
ugf_response: First, we look at how people pick things when they can not see what others do. This is a big problem because if everyone picks a bad path, it hurts all. Then, we think about why this happens. When you cannot see your own doing, you know you did something wrong, so you want to fix it or change it. But when you must do something to get a good thing, like building a house or fixing a broken part, you might still think it is good for you alone. Someone might say that maybe other people just picked it too, but without a way to make sure everyone does the same, the choice becomes hard. So, the key idea is that when there is no clear sign of yourself for doing something, you often pick things up and down in a bad way. So my answer is: When there is not clear signs of who is right or wrong, people often choose in ways that are not perfect or not needed, so they miss out on chances to work together.

english_query: counterexample to: A theodicy T successfully reconciles the existence of a benevolent omnipotent deity with the existence of evil if and only if (i) T provides a morally sufficient reason for the deity's permission of evil, (ii) the reason cited in T is consistent with the deity's omnipotence and benevolence, and (iii) T accounts for the quantity and distribution of evil observed in the world.
ugf_response: First we see that if the other side does not care which of their two moves they will always pick the choice that gives them the most good thing even if it hurts the plan. Then we note that when both sides care about their own best move they cannot go back to work together because each would rather get a worse end than a fight. Next we think that if one side thinks the other might do a bad move they may still try to get more so the bad move can be seen as the best answer. But someone could say that maybe they really care about the group and want to help the other so they should still go for the bad move. Even so without a way to stop that care the fear of losing the chance to help makes the idea less strong. So my answer is: So the best answer is that the game shows why trust matters; the good end from working together stays the better answer.

english_query: counterexample to: A topological space X is connected if and only if (i) X cannot be partitioned into two non-empty disjoint open sets, (ii) any two points in X can be joined by a continuous path lying entirely within X, and (iii) every continuous function from X to the integers is constant.
ugf_response: First, we look at how people pick their moves when they cannot talk for sure. This happens when everyone agrees on one move and one person decides not to change their mind. If this group of choices cannot happen, the choice is easy because there are no other ways to act. Then, we think about why this matters. It shows that no single person has all the power over the others. Some might say that picking a leader is good enough to work, but if you cannot trust the plan, you will have big problems later. So, the group stays stuck because the need to agree is bigger than the need to make the best choice. So my answer is: When players cannot speak or force a choice, they often stop using those who want to do well to avoid trouble, which stops them from working together.

english_query: counterexample to: A translation T of a sentence S from language L1 into language L2 is adequate if and only if (i) T preserves the truth-conditions of S, (ii) T is grammatically well-formed in L2, and (iii) T employs vocabulary in L2 that corresponds to the vocabulary of S in L1.
ugf_response: First we see that words can have many parts. One way says Share some thing. Another way says Keep all thing. So the part that needs a lot is not the whole thing. Then we think about what matters: the good things of hearing people and things. If people get more than they give they may be seen as not right. That can make the talk end bad. But someone might say that the part that need a lot still has help from other people. That is true, but we also think about how people feel. So we should look at both the good part and the bad part of each word before we decide if the talk ends good or bad. In short, the part that uses words only works when it shows real help and does not take away help from other points. So my answer is: The best answer is to see the part that lets us show real help while keeping other parts not taking help.

english_query: counterexample to: An argument A for the existence of a divine being is sound if and only if (i) A proceeds from premises that are known to be true by reason alone, (ii) A employs only valid deductive inferences, and (iii) A arrives at the conclusion that a being possessing all perfections exists.
ugf_response: First we see that people who pick things can not agree on one thing because each person may want something different about it. Then we think that a group must have a way where the choice of the first thing beats the second thing when asked in a clear way. Next we note that this way does not let any idea move away from what most people would say is okay. So the group can end up with a state where most people like the first thing more than the second thing, which stays as a place where everyone agrees. Some might say a change could still happen but without such a change the group cannot find a single clear best choice. Even if it looks simple the problem of having such a changing view stays because no one has the final answer. So my answer is: The state of just order means that no single choice gets every feeling safe; the state of a place where most people agree can still go out.

english_query: counterexample to: A region has a particular climate if and only if (i) the average temperature and precipitation are stable over a long period, (ii) the region can be assigned to a recognised climatic zone, and (iii) the climatic conditions are determined by the region's latitude and altitude.
ugf_response: First, we look at how a plan is good if it helps people than hurts them. If one way gives more good stuff in all, it wins. This means no way is left behind just for that one time. Second, we think about what happens when we do not know what will happen next. If we pick the wrong way, there is no sure thing we will get, so picking the bad way leads to a bad end for everyone. Third, we ask if there is any way to avoid being made to lose by a lie. If someone says you cannot take a lie, then your word does not matter. But even if that person believes you will, they might still want to hurt you anyway. This fear of losing everything stops us from picking the best ways. Finally, we see that this way is only strong when the world changes, and in some cases, hiding your choice does not fix it. So my answer is: The idea is true because if you hide your choice while others take it, you lose out on good things and lose trust, making your promise to stay safe bad.

english_query: counterexample to: A set of sentences is consistent if and only if there exists a model that satisfies all members of the set, no sentence in the set is the negation of another, and the set is finite.
ugf_response: First, we look at how groups change over time. When people have more than half the group, they often feel left out because they think it is not right for them alone. This is a problem where everyone thinks they are doing well. Then, we see what happens when a few people have a different idea about what is good. They might pick by chance or who made up a cause. Even though the bad thing happened by chance, most people still think it is okay to try. So, the set of plans breaks down because there is no shared cause or reason for each person to do something different from the others. Someone might say that having more choices makes it easier to act fast, but moving fast does not help if the world has many small parts. The strong answer is that the system fails to save time on its own good things. So my answer is: When groups share things and care for one person without asking why, the ways that groups stay together fail to keep things working well.

english_query: counterexample to: A political arrangement P is legitimate if and only if (i) P derives its authority from the consent of the governed, (ii) the consent is given freely and without coercion, and (iii) the governed have a genuine opportunity to withdraw their consent if the arrangement fails to serve their interests.
ugf_response: First we see that we need to find which thing is best. Then we look at each possible problem and ask if there are two things that can happen: whether the place where people drink is safe or not. Next we think about how often each problem might happen: if it could happen a lot we might want it more than if it does not happen much, but we must also think about how sure each problem is. Then each problem may be different because one problem may hide another problem. After we look at the facts we have for each problem. Some people say we should just pick the thing that seems most probably good because our feeling about chance is strong. Others say we should try to look at all the chances and use numbers to pick the thing with the higher number. Finally we check if any other thing can make the place where money is kept go down. So my answer is: To decide whose things matter most we need to look at side by side the choices: change each problem, look at what happens each problem shows, look at side in other cases, and try to look past only those.

english_query: counterexample to: A person P acquires title to land by prescription if and only if (i) P has been in continuous and uninterrupted possession of the land for the requisite statutory period, (ii) P's possession has been open and notorious — that is, visible to the world and not concealed, and (iii) P's possession has been adverse to the interests of the true owner — that is, without the true owner's permission.
ugf_response: First, we look at the person who leads the boat. This person says they have done good work and will not give up because they are the one with power. This makes others trust them more because they know they are good people. Then, we think about what happens when someone breaks the law. The leader stops them from talking to the leaders. These people might say bad things about the leader. But if the leader does the right thing now, the group can fix the bad parts. Everyone knows that doing bad things is not allowed. So, even though there was no real law, the leader still did well because they helped keep order. Some might say people can break laws without being watched. But usually, laws are hard to change. So the leader makes it hard to be seen as a bad person. So my answer is: The leader does not do well on the law; he just keeps the promise that his group is safe from trouble.
